claims.text: Nostradamus predicted a global famine worse than any in history that will disrupt social hierarchies and state power.

evidence.quote:
greater trouble is coming and that greater trouble is supposed to take
the form of a global famine

claims.rationale: Host later reveals none of the specific prophecy attributions are trustworthy (many 9/11-style lines were fabricated post-event). Speculative framing of a contemporary famine tied to Nostradamus is not evidentially grounded.

claims.text: Nostradamus predicted California will be destroyed by a massive earthquake.

evidence.quote: California will be destroyed by a massive earthquake

claims.rationale: The specific Thanksgiving 2021 California-destroyed-by-earthquake prediction the host names did not happen — it passed years before this video's upload. Host's broader verdict classifies this kind of Nostradamus attribution as fabricated.

claims.text: Some websites claim Nostradamus's predictions have been about 70 percent accurate.

evidence.quote: Well, some websites say he's been 70 percent accurate

claims.rationale: Host reports the claim but spends the second half of the video dismantling it ('nobody has ever used Nostradamus writings to predict a future event'). No peer-reviewed backing for the 70% figure; it's a clickbait statistic.

claims.text: Nostradamus accurately predicted the 1559 jousting death of King Henry II of France.

evidence.quote:
Henry, the second king of
France, who was only 40 when he died, jousting in fifteen fifty nine

claims.rationale: Henry II's 1559 jousting death is real history, but host explicitly debunks this specific prediction in the verdict section — the 'young lion' quatrain lines were added years after Henry's death.

claims.text: Nostradamus predicted the 1666 Great Fire of London.

evidence.quote:
Great Fire of London in sixteen sixty
six where thousands of people were killed.

claims.rationale: The '23 three 6 = 1666' math is the classic motivated-reasoning decode Nostradamus enthusiasts perform. Host's verdict section argues none of these 'accurate predictions' stand up to scrutiny.

claims.text: Nostradamus accurately foresaw the rise of Hitler.

evidence.quote:
rise of Hitler from the depths of the west of
Europe, a young child will be born of poor people.
Hitler was born in Austri

claims.rationale: Host later reveals Erika Cheatham reinterpreted 'Hister' (a Danube tributary Nostradamus knew well) as 'Hitler' — motivated-translation rather than genuine prophecy.

claims.text: Nostradamus's 'steel birds fall from the sky on the metropolis' quatrain predicted the 9/11 attacks.

evidence.quote:
added after the 9/11 attacks and just became part of the folklore, even
the quatrains about King Henry's death.
You know,

claims.rationale: Host explicitly debunks: the 9/11 'steel birds' lines were fabricated after September 11, 2001 and attributed to Nostradamus as a hoax. Near-zero truth.

claims.text: Nostradamus's writings have never been used to successfully predict a future event before it happened.

evidence.quote:
truth is, nobody has ever used Nostradamus writings to predict a future event.
Never one time that ever once, n

claims.rationale: Host's explicit verdict. Strong claim but supported by the history of Nostradamus interpretation: all 'accurate' predictions are retrodictive (fitted after the event), never prospectively demonstrated.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Directly refutes the 70% accuracy claim — if no one has ever used his writings to predict an event, the accuracy rate is definitionally zero.
9/11 'prediction' is the archetypal case of the retrodictive pattern this claim identifies.

claims.text: Nostradamus's prophecies were virtually unknown during his lifetime; his contemporary fame came from cookbooks and almanacs.

evidence.quote: Nostradamus prophecies were virtually unknown during his lifetime

claims.rationale: Historically, Nostradamus's Les Propheties (1555) circulated within elite and royal circles but only gained mass readership posthumously via 17th-century editions. Host's 'virtually unknown' is a mild overstatement but directionally correct.

claims.text: Nostradamus's prophecies were written as rhyming quatrains in middle French, not in a secret code.

evidence.quote:
prophecies were not called the centuries.
They were called the prophecies.
They were not written in code.
They

claims.rationale: Verifiable from the 1555 text. The quatrains are rhymed middle-French verse, not cipher; obscurity is interpretive not encrypted. Host corrects a common misconception.

claims.text: Nostradamus was never placed on the Vatican's Index of Forbidden Books and was actually on good terms with the Catholic Church.

evidence.quote:
He made no discoveries about the solar system and his only alleged contact with the
Inquisition was an invitatio

claims.rationale: Nostradamus does not appear in the Index Librorum Prohibitorum records. He died in 1566, decades before the post-Reformation intensification of the Index; host's verdict on this point matches modern biographical scholarship.

claims.text: The apparent success of Nostradamus prophecies is driven by flexible translation and interpretation, letting interpreters fit any story to the quatrains.

evidence.quote:
You can make any story fit if you want to be.
Nostradamus talks about a great lady.
Does he mean a literal lady or does h

claims.rationale: Standard debunking framework for prophecy claims — the Barnum / forer-effect + retrodictive translation explains why vague quatrains appear prescient. Matches mainstream skeptical analysis.

dependencies.rationale: The translation-flexibility mechanism is exactly why no prospective prediction has ever been demonstrated.

claims.text: Erika Cheatham was an influential Nostradamus interpreter who reinterpreted his 'Hister' reference as 'Hitler'.

evidence.quote:
Erika Cheatham.
She's best known for reinterpreting Nostradamus reference to history as a reference to
Hitler.
But his d

claims.rationale: Erika Cheetham's The Prophecies of Nostradamus (1973) is real and made exactly this interpretive move on the Hister/Danube passage. Well-documented in Nostradamus studies.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Hister (Danube tributary) reinterpreted as Hitler by Cheetham shows the Hitler connection is interpreter-supplied, not genuine prophecy.

claims.text: Humans are psychologically drawn to prophecies because they suggest disasters are part of a larger plan that could not have been avoided.

evidence.quote:
The problem is none of it's true.
What? Now that we've covered the my

claims.rationale: Host's closing psychological framing (pattern-seeking, locus of control, apocalyptic comfort) is well supported by cognitive-science research on apophenia and motivated reasoning. Evidence quote is a transition sentence because his explicit psychology passage is late in the verdict.
